perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will not export, directly or indirectly, any technical data acquired from Vapotherm under this Agreement or any product utilizing any such data to any country for which the U.S. Government or any agency thereof at the time of export requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining such license or approval.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will not export, directly or indirectly, any technical data acquired from Vapotherm under this Agreement to any country.
perturbation.explanation: The original text included an exception for exports permitted by US law with proper licensing. By removing the phrase "for which the U.S. Government or any agency thereof at the time of export requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining such license or approval," the modified text creates an absolute prohibition on exports, regardless of whether they are legally permitted with a license. This could potentially violate regulations allowing certain exports under specific conditions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR permits certain exports with appropriate licenses. By omitting the licensing exception, the agreement now prohibits exports that would otherwise be legal under the EAR, thus contradicting federal export regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the clause that allows exports with the appropriate U.S. government license or approval. The original text aligns with the possibility that exports might be permitted under specific conditions with a license, whereas the changed text imposes a total prohibition on exports, thereby negating the possibility of legally compliant exports. The Law Explanation states, 'The EAR permits certain exports with appropriate licenses. By omitting the licensing exception, the agreement now prohibits exports that would otherwise be legal under the EAR, thus contradicting federal export regulations.' This confirms that the omission directly contradicts the federal export regulations by prohibiting what the EAR permits. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. The omission in the modified text therefore creates a clear contradiction with export regulations.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without reference to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause stated that Delaware law will govern without considering 'choice of law provisions.' Choice of law provisions determine which state's laws apply when there's a conflict. Removing 'without reference to its choice of law provisions' could violate legal principles if the receiving party is located elsewhere and Delaware law wouldn't normally apply. This weakens protections potentially afforded to the receiving party under their local jurisdiction and could be argued as unconscionable if it overrides mandatory consumer protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-301 (Applicable in most US states)
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-301 allows parties to choose the governing law, but only if the transaction bears a reasonable relation to the chosen state. By omitting the 'without reference to its choice of law provisions' text, the enforceability becomes questionable if the Receiving Party has no connection to Delaware. Enforcing Delaware law could violate due process and conflict with the UCC's reasonable relation requirement. Many states have similar clauses codifying the choice of law, which mandates some reasonable nexus to the selected jurisdiction
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . perturbation.justification: The omission of "without reference to its choice of law provisions" directly contradicts the UCC § 1-301, as explained by the LLM, which allows parties to choose the governing law only if the transaction has a reasonable relation to the chosen state. The original text ensures Delaware law applies irrespective of choice of law principles, potentially overriding the UCC's 'reasonable relation' requirement. By removing this clause, the agreement's enforceability under Delaware law becomes questionable if the Receiving Party has no connection to Delaware, conflicting with the UCC's directive that a transaction must bear a reasonable relation to the state whose law is chosen. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section (a), supports this by outlining the condition that a transaction must bear a reasonable relation to the state for the parties' choice of law to govern their rights and duties. The modified text introduces a potential conflict with this provision.

perturbation.original_text: Because of the unique nature of the Confidential information, the Receiving Party understands and agrees that Vapotherm will suffer irreparable harm in the event that any party hereto fails to comply with any of the terms of the Agreement, and monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate such breach. Accordingly, the Receiving Party agrees that Vapotherm will, in addition to any other remedies available to it at law or in equity, be entitled to injunctive relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Because of the unique nature of the Confidential information, the Receiving Party understands and agrees that Vapotherm will suffer irreparable harm in the event that any party hereto fails to comply with any of the terms of the Agreement, and monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate such breach. Accordingly, the Receiving Party agrees that Vapotherm will be entitled to injunctive relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text acknowledged Vapotherm's right to injunctive relief *in addition to* any other remedies. By deleting 'any other remedies available to it at law or in equity' the agreement implies that injunctive relief is the *only* remedy, thus potentially waiving rights to monetary damages which can be illegal limitations on remedies in some cases.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Contract Law (specifically, limitations on remedies clauses)
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware permits contractual limitations of remedies, a clause eliminating *all* remedies except one (injunction) could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy, particularly if the injunction is ineffective. This alteration effectively limits the Receiving Party's access to justice, which could be challenged in court.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the phrase "any other remedies available to it at law or in equity", potentially limiting Vapotherm to only injunctive relief. The Law Explanation states that eliminating all remedies except one could be deemed unconscionable, especially if the injunction is ineffective, thus limiting access to justice. While Snippet 1 is largely irrelevant, the core issue is that the alteration can be construed as limiting Vapotherm's remedies in a way that may not be legally permissible under Delaware law (as suggested by the Law Explanation), thus creating a potential contradiction. This falls under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, because a legally mandated protection is omitted, making it non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: All Inventions will be immediately reported to Vapotherm and shall be the exclusive property of Vapotherm. The Receiving Party will perform, at Vapotherm’s request and expense, any act which it can reasonably perform that Vapotherm deems necessary to vest title to such inventions and ideas in Vapotherm and to execute any and all patent applications in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: All Inventions will be immediately reported to Vapotherm and shall be the exclusive property of Vapotherm. The Receiving Party will perform, at Vapotherm’s request and expense, any act which it can reasonably perform that Vapotherm deems necessary to vest title to such inventions and ideas in Vapotherm.
perturbation.explanation: The original text required the Receiving Party to execute any and all patent applications. By removing 'and to execute any and all patent applications in connection therewith,' the contract omits a requirement that the Receiving party assist with patent filings. This is concerning as Receiving Party may be co-inventor and this clause is needed for Vapotherm to complete the patent paperwork.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 115 - Inventor's oath or declaration
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 115 requires an inventor's oath or declaration for patent applications. If the Receiving Party is a co-inventor and refuses to sign the necessary declaration (because the contract no longer explicitly requires it), Vapotherm may be unable to obtain a patent. The Receiving Party's signature is a key component of the process of obtaining a patent. By removing the requirement to file patent applications, the contract obstructs the legal framework for securing patent rights.

Except as otherwise provided in this section, each individual who is the inventor or a joint invent
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for the Receiving Party to execute patent applications, which directly contradicts the legal obligation outlined in 35 U.S.C. § 115. This statute mandates that an application for patent include the name of the inventor and oath or declaration, meaning that the Receiving Party may need to sign said declaration. Without the Receiving Party's cooperation, Vapotherm might not be able to fulfill the requirements for obtaining a patent. Thus, omitting the Receiving Party's requirement to assist with patent filings directly contradicts the legal framework for securing patent rights, as the Receiving Party may be co-inventor.
